title: Bloch Quantum Tech Hub Secures $55M in Federal and Matching Grants to Build U.S. Quantum Supply Chain - Quantum Computing Report
author: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
text:
Bloch Quantum Tech Hub Secures $55M in Federal and Matching Grants to Build U.S. Quantum Supply Chain

The U.S. Department of Commerce’s Economic Development Administration (EDA) has selected The Bloch Quantum Tech Hub for a $30 million federal implementation award. Managed by the Chicago Quantum Exchange (CQE), the multi-state consortium spans Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin—a region referred to as the “Quantum Prairie”.

The federal award has unlocked an additional $25 million in state and private matching funds, bringing the total new capital deployment to $55 million. The initiative is designed to transition the domestic quantum sector from lab-scale prototypes to industrial-scale manufacturing, directly reducing reliance on foreign-sourced hardware components and materials.

Mitigating Foreign Supply Chain Dependencies

While the United States maintains strong leadership in quantum software, algorithm design, and early QPU architecture, much of the underlying physical supply chain—such as specialized cryogenic components, ultra-pure optical materials, and precision control subsystems—remains reliant on overseas imports.

The $55 million funding block will be distributed across four interconnected operational tracks to build a resilient domestic manufacturing ecosystem:

Manufacturing Transition Services: Providing technical assistance funds, shared sensing testbeds at Purdue University Northwest, and automation software via Infleqtion to help quantum startups transition to scaled manufacturing.

Industrial Supply Chain Integration: Led by the Illinois Manufacturing Excellence Center (IMEC), this track connects traditional Midwest manufacturing firms with quantum developers, establishing quality standards for domestic suppliers.

Security & Capital Mobilization: Establishing security protocols for U.S. quantum IP and mobilizing an estimated $300 million in private follow-on investment.

Infrastructure Buildout (“The FaQtory”): Funding the construction of a dedicated supplier building within the Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park (IQMP). The state of Illinois is contributing nearly $11 million toward this facility, co-locating component suppliers alongside anchor tenants like PsiQuantum and IBM.

Bipartisan Backing for Regional Deep-Tech Leadership

The Bloch is one of six regional innovation hubs awarded implementation funding under the EDA’s competitive Tech Hubs program, which was originally established under the CHIPS and Science Act.

By bringing together world-leading research universities (including the University of Chicago and Argonne National Laboratory), regional state leadership, and industrial partners, the program leverages the Midwest’s existing manufacturing base to accelerate commercialization timelines.

Over the coming decade, The Bloch is projected to drive significant regional economic impact, expanding skilled technical jobs in advanced manufacturing, cryogenic assembly, and quantum hardware engineering across Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin.

Review the official federal program overview via the U.S. Economic Development Administration Announcement here, and examine regional legislative reactions via Senator Todd Young’s Office here and the Office of Governor JB Pritzker here. For complete multi-state consortium details, project breakdowns, and regional supply chain maps, explore the comprehensive coverage on the UChicago Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering here, read the full press release at the Chicago Quantum Exchange Newsroom here, and consult the central hub overview here.

July 20, 2026
